dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: This work date must not be inferred from the broader date of the Laud codex or the dates of its hands.
dateStatements.value: End of the thirteenth century; precise dating disputed

entities.description: The conceptual anonymous Middle English verse romance, distinct from its physical witnesses and later editions, encodings, scans, and descriptions. DIMEV identifies it as record 1795 / IMEV-NIMEV 1114 and gives the conventional extent as 3,001 lines in rhyming couplets.
entities.label: Anonymous Middle English The Lay of Havelok the Dane
notes: The related Anglo-Norman lai and Gaimar episode belong to the wider Havelok tradition, but no particular French manuscript is asserted as the English poem's physical exemplar.

rights.basis: The work was composed in the medieval period and is far outside any subsisting copyright term; this conclusion does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, descriptions, scans, or photographs.
rights.component: Anonymous Middle English verbal work
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where the medieval copyright term has expired

rights.component: Checked-in XML artifact as a copy of the U-M encoding
notes: Treat the medieval verbal text and historical edition as public domain while keeping the copied encoding layer's status explicit and unresolved.

entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2626
notes: The carrier contains four DIV1 elements, 222 PB, 223 EPB, 97 LG, 2,827 L, 135 NOTE1, 63 MILESTONE, and eight P elements.

Of 2,823 direct LG/L positions, two are asterisk-only lacuna rows; the remaining 2,821 are extant verbal medieval verse lines. Four additional L elements occur inside two editorial notes and are not part of the direct poem stream.

The conventional line sequence reaches 3,001 while lines 1445–1624 are absent because of the lost Laud leaf. The 97 LG containers represent Skeat's editorial paragraph or display groups, not 97 intrinsic stanzas.

rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense file phrased for software, but the review did not find an explicit statement that it licenses the imported U-M XML corpus or overrides any upstream encoding rights.

bibliographic.title: The Lay of Havelok the Dane

entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding: an EETS re-edition of Madden's 1828 volume whose English poem Skeat also checked directly against Laud Misc. 108 twice.
entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Lay of Havelok the Dane (1868)
notes: Skeat's direct manuscript work and his scoped inheritance from Madden are modeled separately; neither relation should be erased in a flattened one-parent chain.
Skeat omitted the complete Anglo-Norman lai and Gaimar text printed by Madden, retaining only selected comparison passages in notes.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1868 and is outside any possible United States copyright term.
rights.component: Skeat's 1868 editorial text and apparatus
notes: This status does not automatically determine rights in the Internet Archive scan derivative.

bibliographic.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane, accompanied by the French Text, with an Introduction, Notes, and a Glossary (London: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club, 1828).

bibliographic.publisher: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club
bibliographic.title: The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane

entities.description: The first printed edition of the Middle English poem, transcribed directly from Laud Misc. 108 and published with an introduction, French analogues, notes, and glossary.
entities.label: Frederic Madden, The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane (1828)
notes: Madden reports permission to transcribe Laud and says he adhered scrupulously to its orthography while supplying punctuation, word division, capitalization, obvious letter corrections, and modern equivalents for specified archaic letterforms.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1828 and is outside any possible United States copyright term.
rights.component: Madden's 1828 editorial text, introduction, notes, and glossary
notes: This status does not automatically license every later scan derivative or provider metadata record.

access.accessMethod: Follow Bodleian Special Collections reader procedures; the catalog directs readers to use the digital reproduction because consultation of the original is restricted.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
notes: No onsite consultation was undertaken for this record.

dateStatements.label: containing codex
dateStatements.notes: The Havelok/King Horn hand is more narrowly described as late thirteenth or early fourteenth century.

entities.description: The sole substantially complete witness of Havelok, copied by hand C at ff. 204ra–219va. One lost leaf after f. 211 removes conventional lines 1445–1624. King Horn begins later in the same f. 219va column.

holding.notes: Havelok occupies ff. 204ra–219va; the containing production unit continues with King Horn.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108

physicalDescription.extent: Havelok at ff. 204ra–219va within a complete composite codex; one leaf missing after f. 211
physicalDescription.format: Two columns, normally 45 lines; hand C
notes: The Havelok/King Horn scribe's language is associated with west Norfolk; this is witness dialect, not the poem's Northeast Midlands composition localization.
The codex's possible Oxford origin is tentative and must not be promoted to the work's place of composition.

access.accessMethod: No complete public surrogate was located in Cambridge Digital Library, Fragmentarium, general-web, or plausible CUDL-IIIF searches; ask Cambridge to confirm whether an unpublished or differently cataloged surrogate exists.
notes: This is a dated negative search finding, not a claim that the item has never been digitized.

access.accessMethod: Submit the Cambridge Imaging Services online form or email photo@lib.cam.ac.uk. Request the complete physical item: all four scraps, all eight surfaces, housing or envelope, edges, and a scale or color target where offered—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.
access.format: Professional digital images; exact specification and licence by quotation
notes: The published minimum charge is £25 excluding VAT; Cambridge states that quotations are normally supplied within about two weeks.
Images are initially supplied for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation; reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.

rights.basis: The item has not yet been ordered, so no image-specific licence exists in this record. Cambridge states that supplied images are initially for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation and that reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.
rights.component: Any professional images supplied by Cambridge Imaging Services
notes: Record the exact quotation and licence before redistributing any supplied images in the repository, a publication, or a commercial print-on-demand product.

access.accessMethod: Contact Cambridge University Library Special Collections in advance to determine current reader and handling conditions for the fragment item.
access.format: Four paper binding scraps / eight surfaces
notes: No onsite consultation was undertaken for this record.

dateStatements.label: fragment witness
dateStatements.notes: Skeat's 1911 end-of-fourteenth-century estimate is retained as publication history but superseded here by the modern cataloging date.

entities.description: A binding-fragment item consisting of four physical paper scraps and eight surfaces, conventionally lettered a–h. Three scraps preserve Havelok text on surfaces d, e, and f; the fourth physical scrap is part of the complete item but does not carry Havelok.

holding.notes: Request the complete fragment item—all four scraps and both sides—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19)

physicalDescription.extent: Four physical scraps / eight surfaces; Havelok survives on three scraps, surfaces d–f
notes: DIMEV maps d to conventional lines 174–183, e to 341–364, and f to 537–549.
Skeat's transcription preserves eleven verse lines absent from the Laud witness: ten after line 544 and one in the following passage.

rights.basis: The item has not yet been ordered, so no image-specific licence exists in this record. Cambridge states that supplied images are initially for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation and that reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.
rights.component: Any professional images supplied by Cambridge Imaging Services
notes: Record the exact quotation and licence before redistributing any supplied images in the repository, a publication, or a commercial print-on-demand product.

workPortion.label: Havelok within the complete Laud Misc. 108 manuscript bundle
locators: canvas 454 / f. 219v / end and King Horn boundary surface 54829840-bf61-4ed1-ba6e-cd1c775a1474

next_steps: Consult G. V. Smithers's 1987 critical edition directly and review the legal, historical, linguistic, and paleographical arguments surveyed by Thomas Liszka.
Retain the broad specialist-repertory end-of-thirteenth-century display until primary scholarly arguments justify narrower bounds.
openQuestions.question: How narrowly should Havelok's composition date be stated within the late-thirteenth- to early-fourteenth-century debate?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge to confirm whether a nonpublic or differently identified digital surrogate exists.
If not, request professional color images of all four physical scraps, all eight surfaces, housing or envelope, edges, and scale or color target where offered.
Record exact image-supply and reproduction licence terms before redistribution.
notes: The negative finding is bounded to a dated scoped search and is not an assertion that the item has never been digitized.
openQuestions.question: Does Cambridge University Library hold an unpublished or differently cataloged complete digital surrogate of Add. 4407 (19)?

next_steps: Recheck the stable U-M item after the Cloudflare interstitial is no longer returned.
Ask U-M Library whether the collection-level public-domain statement extends to TEI markup and bulk XML redistribution.

next_steps: Obtain a written quotation and proposed licence for the complete four-scrap item.
State the intended repository, scholarly-reference, and possible downstream commercial or print-on-demand context explicitly when seeking permission.

reviewNotes: Primary transmission paths preserve both Skeat's direct Laud work and his scoped dependence on Madden; the graph should not force a single exclusive parent.
Supporting descriptions and the 1911 Cambridge transcription are outside the direct artifact-to-witness path.

All cached supporting-only catalog, metadata, scandata, and scholarship carriers are marked target_work_presence absent. Actual editions, OCR texts, article text, and manuscript images that contain Havelok are marked present with locators.

lineage.summary: The checked-in aha2626 XML is a copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item AHA2626.0001.001. That electronic item encodes Walter W. Skeat's 1868 edition of the Middle English poem on printed pp. 1–86. Skeat described his volume as a revised re-edition of Frederic Madden's 1828 first edition, retained Madden's text as his editorial base, checked proofs against Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 twice, and incorporated Madden's later manuscript corrections. Madden had transcribed the manuscript directly. Laud Misc. 108, ff. 204ra–219va, is the sole substantially complete witness; one leaf is missing after f. 211 and conventional lines 1445–1624 are lost. Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19) consists of four physical paper scraps, three of which preserve collateral portions of Havelok; Skeat first published those fragments in 1911, so they are not upstream of either the 1828 or 1868 edition. A complete Digital Bodleian facsimile of Laud is cached, and its exact Havelok range is mapped within the whole manuscript. No public facsimile of the complete Cambridge fragment item was found in a dated scoped search; Cambridge Imaging Services can be asked to supply all four scraps and eight surfaces. Catalogs, repertories, modern scholarship, scans, OCR derivatives, access conditions, and rights layers are modeled separately from the physical and textual transmission path.

catalogingSubject.label: The Lay of Havelok the Dane
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse romance conventionally titled The Lay of Havelok the Dane. The cataloging subject is the medieval work represented by the substantially complete text in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 and by collateral portions on three of the four physical scraps in Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19). It is not the whole Laud codex, either physical witness, the related Anglo-Norman and chronicle traditions, Frederic Madden's 1828 edition, Walter W. Skeat's 1868 re-edition or 1911 fragment transcription, the University of Michigan encoding, its page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

contentParts.label: Skeat/U-M representation of the substantially complete Laud text

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; 2,823 direct LG/L positions, of which 2 are asterisk-only lacuna rows and 2,821 are extant verbal verse lines
AHA2626.0001.001; printed pp. 1–86; conventional lines 1–3001 with lines 1445–1624 absent
ff. 204ra–219va; missing quire-19 leaf after f. 211 causes loss of conventional lines 1445–1624; Havelok ends before King Horn begins in the same f. 219va column
canvases 423–454 of 496 / bundle images/000423.jpg–images/000454.jpg; lacuna boundary canvases 438–439; shared final f. 219v canvas 454

notes: The 97 encoded LG containers are representation metrics only. Skeat states that the manuscript marks paragraphs with large initials and that he inserted slight spaces at those points; group sizes vary too widely to support 97 intrinsic stanzas.

Four additional L elements occur inside two editorial NOTE1 notes and contain conjectural supplied verse. They are editorial apparatus, not part of the direct poem stream or the 2,821-line intellectual-content count.
The collateral Cambridge fragment witness preserves eleven verse lines absent from Laud. Those readings are witness variants and are not silently inserted into this Skeat/U-M source representation.
contentParts.summary: The poem as printed by Skeat in 1868 and encoded by U-M: conventional lines 1–3001, with lines 1445–1624 absent after the loss of one Laud leaf. The encoded direct poem stream has 2,821 extant verbal lines; it does not incorporate the eleven additional verse lines first published from the Cambridge fragments in 1911.

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives the end of the thirteenth century. Madden proposed 1270–1290; the modern dating survey records influential arguments extending the possible composition window into the opening decade of the fourteenth century. No exact year is normalized.

dateStatements.display: End of the thirteenth century; precise date disputed

assertion.notes: The normalized 1290–1325 interval maps the catalog's not-before 1290 and its late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century Hand C description. The containing codex's separately recorded broader 1290–1400 bounds are not used as the work's attestation interval. This is an attestation date, not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century in Laud Misc. 108

scope.description: Date bounds of the containing codex; the Havelok/King Horn hand is more narrowly described as late thirteenth or early fourteenth century
First printing of the rediscovered Middle English poem

extent.description: One verse romance in the immediate Skeat/U-M source representation. A raw XML count finds 2,827 L elements: 2,823 are direct children of the 97 LG containers, while four conjectural verse lines are nested inside two editorial NOTE1 notes. Of the 2,823 direct positions, two are asterisk-only display rows after conventional line 1444, leaving 2,821 extant verbal medieval verse lines. Conventional editorial numbering runs through 3001; 180 positions, lines 1445–1624, are absent because one manuscript leaf is lost. The final unnumbered direct L is conventional line 3001. These figures describe the Laud-based 1868/U-M representation, not a critical total across both witnesses; the Cambridge fragments preserve eleven additional lines absent from Laud. The carrier audit separately finds four DIV1 elements, 222 PB elements, 223 EPB elements, 97 LG elements, 135 NOTE1 elements, 63 manuscript MILESTONE elements, and eight P elements. The four DIV1 containers comprise one omitted-half-title placeholder, one omitted-front-matter placeholder, the poem, and one omitted-back-matter placeholder. The 222 PB/223 EPB scaffold spans the digitized 1868 carrier, while only the title page, poem, and inline notes are verbally encoded. The 97 LGs correspond to Skeat's editorial paragraph/display spaces rather than intrinsic stanzas, so sections remains null and verse_groups is retained only as an encoding-layer metric.

formStatements.description: A narrative poem in short rhyming couplets, described by the Database of Middle English Romance as aa4 bb4 and by DIMEV as two-line couplets. The 97 LG containers in the U-M XML reproduce Skeat's editorial paragraph/display spacing and are not evidence for 97 intrinsic stanzas.

assertion.notes: Bell and Couch describe the established Matter of England classification while emphasizing that Laud's hagiographical context complicates a purely secular reading.
The poem frames legendary dynastic restoration against English and Danish history; the label does not assert that its events are historical fact.
Religious invocations, providential signs, and Laud's vita rubric remain important, but the primary modern classification is romance rather than a saint's life.

notes: Work composition region, Laud scribal dialect, Laud codex origin, and Cambridge fragment origin are kept as separate assertions.

The 1868 title page's historical unique-MS wording is retained as edition history but normalized in this record to sole substantially complete witness because current DIMEV and DMER records include the Cambridge fragments and the Laud text itself has a 180-line physical lacuna.

Rights and practical access for the medieval work, editions, electronic encoding, manuscript photographs, catalogs, and requestable Cambridge images are documented separately in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult G. V. Smithers's 1987 critical edition directly and review the legal, historical, linguistic, and paleographical arguments surveyed by Thomas Liszka.
Retain the specialist repertory's broad end-of-thirteenth-century display until the underlying arguments justify a narrower normalized interval.
openQuestions.question: How should the poem's precise composition date be delimited within the late-thirteenth- to early-fourteenth-century scholarly debate?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library to confirm whether Add. 4407 (19) has an unpublished or differently cataloged digital surrogate.

If no public surrogate exists, request professional color images of the complete physical item—all four scraps, both sides, housing/envelope, edges and scale/color target where offered—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.
Record the exact supplied-image licence before any redistribution or publication.
notes: The dated negative finding covers searches of Cambridge Digital Library, Fragmentarium, the general web, and plausible CUDL IIIF identifiers. It is not a claim that the item has never been digitized.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19), including all four scraps and eight surfaces, available under an identifier not found in the scoped search?

assertion.notes: This specialist-database localization describes the proposed place of composition, not the origin of either surviving physical witness.
The Bodleian record links the Havelok hand to LAEME entry 3 and west Norfolk. This localizes witness language, not the poet.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of hand C in Laud Misc. 108
First edition of the English poem in 1828, directly transcribed from Laud Misc. 108

notes: Skeat read the proof sheets against the manuscript twice; Madden also supplied corrections from his own re-comparison and reviewed the revises.

scope.description: 1868 re-edition, directly checked against Laud Misc. 108 and incorporating Madden's corrected prior edition

subjects.label: Disguise, prophetic dreams, and tokens of recognition
Labor, merchant disguise, and social mobility

summaries.value: Parallel betrayals displace the child heirs of England and Denmark. Earl Goodrich imprisons the English princess Goldborou, while Earl Godard murders Havelok's sisters and orders the fisherman Grim to drown him. Grim recognizes Havelok's royal birthmark and miraculous light, escapes with him to Lincolnshire, and raises him at Grimsby. Havelok later labors in Lincoln, is forced into marriage with Goldborou, and learns through signs and dreams that both are royal heirs. Disguised as merchants, they return to Denmark, win Earl Ubbe's loyalty, defeat Godard, and restore Havelok's Danish kingship. Havelok then returns to England, defeats Goodrich, restores Goldborou, and rules with her after their coronation.

The repository source represents Skeat's 1868 title page, poem on printed pp. 1–86, and inline footnotes. It does not verbally encode his preface, separate notes section, glossary, index, or other omitted carrier matter, although page-break placeholders span the complete scan sequence. Madden's 1828 French lai, Gaimar text, introduction, and apparatus are related edition components rather than parts of the cataloged Middle English work.

Laud Misc. 108 is the sole substantially complete witness, not the only surviving witness. One leaf lost after f. 211 removes conventional lines 1445–1624. Three of the four physical scraps in the mid-fourteenth-century Cambridge fragment item preserve portions corresponding to lines 174–183, 341–364, and 537–549, including eleven lines absent from Laud. The fragments were published only in 1911 and therefore cannot be a source for either Madden's 1828 or Skeat's 1868 edition.

The romance includes child murder, attempted murder, imprisonment, warfare, mob violence, flaying, hanging, burning, and other punitive violence. These features are documented for responsible discovery and interpretation.

assertion.notes: This is a conventional editorial title, not an authorial title preserved at the opening of the poem.

titles.value: The Lay of Havelok the Dane

assertion.notes: The Latin rubric belongs to the witness presentation and does not make the Middle English poem a Latin work or establish a hagiographic genre by itself.

scope.description: Opening two lines in the substantially complete witness tradition

titles.value: Herknet to me, gode men, / Wiues, maydnes, and alle men
Þat ihesu crist his soule bringe / Bi-forn his fader at his endinge

work.summary: Parallel betrayals displace the child heirs of England and Denmark. Earl Goodrich imprisons the English princess Goldborou, while Earl Godard murders Havelok's sisters and orders the fisherman Grim to drown him. Grim recognizes Havelok's royal birthmark and miraculous light, escapes with him to Lincolnshire, and raises him at Grimsby. Havelok later labors in Lincoln, is forced into marriage with Goldborou, and learns through signs and dreams that both are royal heirs. Disguised as merchants, they return to Denmark, win Earl Ubbe's loyalty, defeat Godard, and restore Havelok's Danish kingship. Havelok then returns to England, defeats Goodrich, restores Goldborou, and rules with her after their coronation.
